coredata.dc:title: Intermolecular naphthylamine cyclization–based synthesis of bandgap-modulated carbon dots for multicolor light-emitting diodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Park, Sehyeon
Lee, Su Hwan
Kim, Yujin
Park, Hyeonjin
Kim, Gui-Min
Kim, Young-Hoon
Kwon, Woosung
coredata.dc:description: Owing to their tunable optical properties, N-doped carbon dots (CDs) have a broad scope of optoelectronic applications. However, the difficulty of precisely controlling nitrogen incorporation limits our understanding of its impact on CD electronic structure and emission behavior. To address this gap, N-doped CDs with tunable bandgaps are herein synthesized via the solvothermal carbonization of 1-naphthylamine in the presence of a controlled amount of nitric acid to obtain single-source emitters with two distinct emission wavelengths. The amount of nitric acid determines the extent of nitrogen incorporation through the intermolecular cyclization of 1-naphthylamine and, hence, the proportions of pyrrolic/pyridinic N-heterocycles (red emission, ∼600 nm) and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (blue emission, ∼400 nm). For a practical utility demonstration, the prepared N-doped CDs are used to fabricate high-color-purity multicolor light-emitting diodes. By establishing a correlation between nitrogen incorporation modes and photophysical properties, this study paves the way for the rational design of advanced N-doped CDs for next-generation optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Nitrogen doping
Electronic transition
Dual emission
Light-emitting diode

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Basic Science Program (NRF-2022R1A2C4002403) and the Digital Research Innovation Institution Program (RS-2023-00283597) funded by the National Research Foundation of Korea, Ministry of Science and ICT. This research was partially supported by Korea Basic Science Institute (National research Facilities and Equipment Center) Grant funded by the Ministry of Education (2021R1A6C101A564).

Woosung Kwon reports financial support was provided by National Research Foundation of Korea. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Mid-career Research Grants (NRF-2022R1A2C4002403 and RS-2024-0035615), Digital Research Innovation Institution Program (RS-2023-00283597), Nano & Material Technology Development Program (2021M3H4A3A01062960), and Engineering Research Center (2022R1A5A1033719) funded by the National Research Foundation of Korea, the Ministry of Science and ICT. This research was partially supported by the Korea Basic Science Institute (National research Facilities and Equipment Center) Grant funded by the Ministry of Education (2021R1A6C101A564).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) 1H NMR and (b) FTIR spectra of NA, NA-CD(0), NA-CD(1), and NA-CD(2).
XPS (a–c) C 1s and (d–f) N 1s spectra of (a, d) NA-CD(0), (b, e) NA-CD(1), and (c, f) NA-CD(2).
(a) TEM images of (a) NA-CD(0), (b) NA-CD(1), and (c) NA-CD(2) (scale bar = 5 nm). (d) XRD patterns, (e) Raman spectra, and (f) UV–visible absorption spectra of the NA-CDs.

(a, d, g) PL emission contour maps, (b, e, h) PL emission spectra, and (c, f, i) images of solutions under daylight (left) and UV (365 nm) irradiation (right) obtained for (a–c) NA-CD(0), (d–f) NA-CD(1), and (g–i) NA-CD(2). EX/EM denote excitation/emission in the figures.

TA spectral contour maps of NA-CDs: (a) NA-CD(0), (b) NA-CD(1), and (c) NA-CD(2) recorded at a pump wavelength of 320 nm; (d) NA-CD(0), (e) NA-CD(1), and (f) NA-CD(2) recorded at a pump wavelength of 350 nm.
TRPL spectra of NA-CDs recorded at excitation wavelengths of (a) 310 and (b) 530 nm. (c) Schematic illustration of electronic transitions associated with NISs.

(a) Structure of NA-CD-based LEDs. (b) Energy levels in LEDs. Photographs of LEDs based on (c) NA-CD(0) and (d) NA-CD(2). EL spectra of LEDs based on (e) NA-CD(0) and (f) NA-CD(2) recorded at different operation voltages. The asterisks in (f) indicate the characteristic blue EL of NA-CD(2).
Proposed mechanism for the formation of NA-CDs (grey: carbon, cyan: nitrogen, white: oxygen).

head.ce:title: Intermolecular naphthylamine cyclization–based synthesis of bandgap-modulated carbon dots for multicolor light-emitting diodes

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to their tunable optical properties, N-doped carbon dots (CDs) have a broad scope of optoelectronic applications. However, the difficulty of precisely controlling nitrogen incorporation limits our understanding of its impact on CD electronic structure and emission behavior. To address this gap, N-doped CDs with tunable bandgaps are herein synthesized via the solvothermal carbonization of 1-naphthylamine in the presence of a controlled amount of nitric acid to obtain single-source emitters with two distinct emission wavelengths. The amount of nitric acid determines the extent of nitrogen incorporation through the intermolecular cyclization of 1-naphthylamine and, hence, the proportions of pyrrolic/pyridinic N-heterocycles (red emission, ∼600 nm) and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (blue emission, ∼400 nm). For a practical utility demonstration, the prepared N-doped CDs are used to fabricate high-color-purity multicolor light-emitting diodes. By establishing a correlation between nitrogen incorporation modes and photophysical properties, this study paves the way for the rational design of advanced N-doped CDs for next-generation optoelectronic devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (typically <10 nm in diameter) that exhibit unique optical properties [1–3], excellent photostability, biocompatibility, and minimal toxicity and can therefore find applications in sensing [4], bioimaging [5], drug delivery [6], photocatalysis [7], and optoelectronics [8–10]. The optical (e.g., light absorption and photoluminescence (PL)) properties of CDs arise from their visible-light-region bandgaps of 2–3 eV, which are primarily determined by the presence of sp2-hybridized (graphitic) carbon structures and can be further tuned by controlling surface chemistry, functional groups, and defects [11,12]. The functionality of CDs can be enhanced by manipulating their electronic structure and thus controlling their chemical, optical, and electrical properties [13,14]. Electronic structure modulation typically involves doping with heteroatoms, e.g., boron [15], nitrogen [16,17], oxygen [18], and sulfur [19], to introduce intragap states within the CDs and thus shifts their PL emission wavelengths [20].

Among the heteroatom dopants, nitrogen is a particularly attractive choice [21,22] because its atomic size is similar to that of carbon and thus facilitates the incorporation of the former into the CD structures, while the high electronegativity of nitrogen and its five valence electrons contribute to strong chemical bonding within the CDs. The lone electron pair of nitrogen readily interacts with the π-orbitals of sp2-hybridized carbons to effectively introduce additional photoexcitable electrons into the ground state of CDs [23]. However, despite the demonstrated effectiveness of nitrogen doping in tailoring CD properties, the precise mechanisms underlying nitrogen incorporation during carbonization remain elusive. Consequently, the current methods of synthesizing N-doped CDs often rely on intuition or experience, as opposed to systematic and purposeful design [24,25]. This deficiency impedes in-depth studies on the influence of nitrogen on the electronic structure and optical properties of CDs.

A common and effective nitrogen incorporation strategy involves the formation of pyridine- or pyrrole-like moieties within the CD core, which act as electron donors or acceptors to alter the electronic structure of the CDs [26–29]. This study describes the synthesis of N-doped CDs via the solvothermal carbonization of 1-naphthylamine (NA), referred to as NA-CDs [30], demonstrating that nitrogen incorporation can be controlled by adjusting the amount of the added nitric acid. The employed method leverages the protonation of NA to detach the amine groups as ammonium ions, effectively suppressing the formation of nitrogen-containing heterocycles. The NA-CDs obtained using different nitric acid amounts are characterized in terms of their nitrogen content, bandgap structures, and optoelectronic properties to help establish the design principles for optimizing the optical properties of NA-CDs. Finally, the NA-CDs are used to fabricate highly efficient multicolor light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with excellent color purity and considerable potential for future display applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and chemical characterization of NA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The thermochemical treatment of NA primarily produces N-heterocyclic compounds, such as dibenzoacridine [31]. At high pressure and temperature, NA is oxidatively cleaved to form aminonaphthalic acid [32]. This reaction is driven by the nucleophilic attack of the oxygen atom on the partially positively charged alpha carbon atom adjacent to the amino group of NA. The nucleophilic dehydration of aminonaphthalic acid and subsequent intermolecular cyclization afford a variety of N-heterocycles, such as pyrroles and pyridines. Notably, nitrogen incorporation creates nitrogen-induced intragap states (NISs) significantly influencing the product electronic structure and optical properties. Further carbonization yields N-heterocycle-containing CDs with associated NISs (NA-CD(0)), leading to bandgap reduction and thus facilitating long-wavelength emission.

Intriguingly, nitric acid alters the pathway for the formation of pyridine and pyrrole from NA by protonating the nitrogen atom of the amino group [33]. The subsequent nucleophilic substitution reaction (SN1) results in ammonium ion loss and affords a short-lived phenyl cation, which is converted into a protonated naphthol [34]. The dehydration of the protonated naphthol yields polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons devoid of nitrogen-containing moieties, such as N-heterocycles [35]. Consequently, this process, followed by carbonization, produces CDs with varying degrees of nitrogen incorporation. Specifically, NA-CD(1), prepared using a lower nitric acid loading, incorporates more N-heterocycles and associated NISs compared with NA-CD(2), prepared using a higher nitric acid loading. This difference in nitrogen content and NIS density leads to distinct optical properties. For instance, compared with NA-CD(1), NA-CD(2) favors shorter-wavelength emission, which is characteristic of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. By adjusting the nitric acid loading, we precisely controlled the extent of nitrogen incorporation and, hence, the long-wavelength (∼600 nm) to short-wavelength (∼400 nm) emission ratio. The two formation pathways of NA-CDs are illustrated in Scheme 1 .

1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy was used to probe the structure and composition of NA-CDs (Fig. 1 a and S1). The weak multiplet at 7.2−7.8 ppm (α) was ascribed to aromatic protons, a fundamental structural unit of general CDs. The presence and relative intensity of this peak suggested the formation of a graphitic carbon framework. Additionally, the signal at 5.6 ppm (β) was attributed to protons attached to alkene moieties present at the edges of graphitic carbon domains or within the core structure and contributing to the potential sp2 and sp3 hybridization in the NA-CDs. The peak at 2.5−2.6 ppm (γ) was assigned to primary amine groups and was observed for NA-CD(1) and NA-CD(2) but not for the sample prepared without nitric acid (NA-CD(0)). This observation suggested that nitric acid treatment prevented the complete incorporation of nitrogen into the core structure of the NA-CDs and resulted in the presence of residual amine groups on the surfaces of NA-CD(1) and NA-CD(2). To validate this hypothesis, we synthesized NA-CDs using different time intervals (1−4 h) and analyzed them by 1H NMR spectroscopy (Fig. S2). Consistent with the initial observation, only the spectra of NA-CD(1) and NA-CD(2) displayed primary amine peaks, suggesting that the presence or absence of nitric acid during synthesis directly affected nitrogen incorporation.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used for functional group characterization (Fig. 1b and S3). The spectrum of pristine NA exhibited peaks corresponding to amine N–H stretching (3360 cm−1, i) characteristic of primary amines and aliphatic C–H stretching (2995, 3180, and 3290 cm−1, ii) associated with methylene groups. The disappearance of the aliphatic C–H stretching peaks indicated the transformation of NA to NA-CDs. The emergence of a new peak at 1451 cm−1 (iii) was attributed to the formation of aromatic CC bonds and supported the formation of graphitic carbon within the NA-CDs. Interestingly, the amine N–H stretching peak persisted only in the spectra of NA-CD(1) and NA-CD(2). Consistent with the NMR data, this peak may indicate the presence of residual amine groups on the CD surfaces. This observation further supported the notion that nitric acid treatment hindered the complete incorporation of nitrogen into the core structure of the NA-CDs. Additionally, the presence of peaks at 1210 cm−1 (iv) and 960 cm−1 (v), tentatively assigned to the C–O stretching vibrations of aminonaphthalic acid and O–H stretching vibrations of naphthol, respectively, suggested the formation of intermediates and thus supported the proposed NA-CD synthesis mechanism.

The elemental compositions and chemical environments of the NA-CDs were probed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 2 and S4) to validate the proposed synthesis mechanism (Scheme 1) and assess the influence of nitric acid on nitrogen incorporation. The deconvoluted C 1s spectra of the NA-CDs (Fig. 2a–c and S4a) exhibited three main peaks at 284.8, 286, and 287.7 eV corresponding to C–C/CC, C–N, and CO functionalities, respectively. The prominent C–C/CC peak signified the successful carbonization of NA and formation of polycrystalline (graphitic/amorphous-hybrid) carbon cores within the NA-CDs. The deconvoluted N 1s spectra of the NA-CDs (Fig. 2d–f and S4b) revealed three nitrogen bonding configurations, namely C–N–C (398.1 eV), pyrrolic/pyridinic N (399.64 eV), and other nitrogen species (401.6 eV) [36–38]. With the increasing nitric acid loading, the C–N–C peak (398.1 eV) of NA-CD(1) and NA-CD(2) gained intensity, whereas the corresponding pyrrolic/pyridinic N peak (399.64 eV) lost intensity. This trend suggested that nitric acid treatment suppressed the formation of N-heterocyclic compounds in the NA-CDs. The results of elemental analysis (Table S1) indicated that the nitrogen content of the NA-CDs remained nearly constant across different sample types. This finding suggested that although the total amount of the incorporated nitrogen was constant, its chemical form varied, i.e., N-heterocycles might be the dominant form in NA-CD(0), whereas surface amino groups might be more prevalent in NA-CD(1) and NA-CD(2).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were used to investigate the NA-CD morphology and size. TEM imaging revealed nearly spherical NA-CDs with sizes of 3–6 nm (Fig. 3 a–c) and partially graphitic (crystalline) domains lacking well-defined lattice fringes (Fig. S5). This result suggested the presence of heteroatoms, e.g., oxygen and nitrogen, within the core structure, which disrupted the long-range order typically observed in graphene and other crystalline carbon structures. Consistent with the results of TEM analysis, the DLS-determined particle size distributions were monomodal, with peak diameters of 3.6, 3.9, and 3.4 nm obtained for NA-CD(0), NA-CD(1), and NA-CD(2), respectively (Fig. S6). High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis likewise indicated that all samples exhibited similar retention times, regardless of acid concentration (Fig. S7).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 3d and S8a) of the NA-CDs featured a broad peak at 2θ = 22°, a characteristic signature of graphitic/amorphous-hybrid carbon, which was assigned to the (002) reflection of a disordered graphitic or turbostratic carbon structure [39,40]. The absence of sharp diffraction peaks typically observed for highly crystalline materials indicated a lack of long-range-ordered structures and agreed with the results of TEM analysis, suggesting that the NA-CDs featured a predominantly amorphous structure with potentially small crystalline domains.

The Raman spectra (Fig. 3e and S8b) of the NA-CDs displayed two prominent peaks, namely the D-band (∼1340 cm−1) and G-band (∼1575 cm−1). The former peak was attributed to defects, edge effects, and sp3-hybridized carbons, whereas the latter corresponded to the in-plane vibrations of sp2-hybridized carbons associated with graphitic domains [41]. The corresponding intensity ratios (I D/I G) were close to unity (0.95, 0.99, and 0.99 for NA-CD(0), NA-CD(1), and NA-CD(2), respectively) and suggested a relatively high concentration of defect sites, potentially because of the incorporation of nitrogen atoms and functional groups on the surface [42,43]. The I D/I G variation between NA-CD(0) and NA-CD(1)/NA-CD(2) suggested that nitric acid affected the number of defects or the dimensions of the graphitic domains. The combined results of TEM, DLS, XRD, and Raman spectroscopy analyses suggested that the NA-CDs possessed a turbostratic structure with polycyclic aromatic substructures dispersed in a predominant amorphous carbon matrix with a significant number of defects.

The ultraviolet (UV)–visible absorption spectra (Fig. 3f and S9) displayed sharp primary peaks in the range of 270–310 nm, attributed to π-π∗ transitions within the graphitic carbon structures of NA-CDs, and broader secondary peaks at 510–600 nm, assigned to n-π∗ transitions potentially associated with pyrrole or pyridine functionalities [44,45]. The primary-to-secondary peak intensity ratio increased with the increasing nitric acid loading, which suggested a concomitant increase in the density of graphitic carbon structures or the extent of conjugation and decrease in the content of pyrrole or pyridine structures. This conclusion agreed with the XPS data discussed earlier, namely the decreased N-heterocycle content of NA-CDs prepared in the presence of nitric acid.

The Tauc plot method was used to estimate the optical bandgaps of the NA-CDs (Fig. S10). Specifically, (αhν)2 was plotted against hν (photon energy), where α is the absorption coefficient, h is Planck's constant, and ν is the light frequency [46,47]. The bandgaps of NA-CD(0), NA-CD(1), and NA-CD(2) were determined as 3.68, 3.79, and 3.89 eV, respectively, by extrapolating the linear portion of the plots to the abscissa (photon energy axis). This bandgap increases with the increasing nitric acid loading well aligned with the expected trend. The suppression of N-heterocycle formation, as observed by XPS, probably reduced the introduction of NISs, resulting in bandgap widening with the increasing nitric acid loading.

The PL emission spectra displayed two distinct emission peaks (Fig. 4 and S11). With the increasing nitric acid loading (i.e., suppression of N-heterocycle formation), the short-wavelength peak (390–460 nm) gained intensity, while the long-wavelength peak (560–680 nm) lost intensity. Consequently, the former peak was ascribed to π-π∗ transitions within the graphitic carbon structures, while the latter was attributed to n-π∗ transitions associated with N-heterocycles and the corresponding NISs. This effect was further corroborated by the visually observable change in the PL color of the NA-CD solutions under UV (365 nm) light from red (NA-CD(0), Fig. 4c) to purple (NA-CD(1), Fig. 4f) to violet (NA-CD(2), Fig. 4i). The absolute quantum yields (QYs) of the NA-CDs ranged from 14 % to 34 %, depending on the excitation wavelength (Table S2). NA-CD(0) exhibited the highest QY (33.71 %) when excited at 550 nm. In contrast, NA-CD (1) and (2) exhibited maximum QYs of 20.27 % and 24.96 %, respectively, upon excitation at 310 nm. These results suggested that NA-CD(0), which contained NISs probably originating from N-heterocycles, possessed a narrower bandgap and exhibited a stronger response to long-wavelength excitation (550 nm). Conversely, NA-CD(1) and (2), which lacked NISs owing to nitric acid treatment, exhibited a wider bandgap and more efficiently responded to short-wavelength excitation (310 nm).

Additional experiments were conducted to deeper understand the influence of acid treatment on the properties of the NA-CDs, particularly nitrogen incorporation. Initially, we synthesized an intermediate sample, NA-CD(0.5), using half the nitric acid loading of NA-CD(1) to observe the gradual effect of acid treatment. The properties of NA-CD(0.5) were intermediate between those of NA-CD(0) and NA-CD(1), which strongly suggested that the incorporation of nitrogen in the form of N-heterocycles can be precisely controlled by adjusting the nitric acid loading. Next, we investigated the role of acid strength by introducing sulfuric acid, which is stronger than nitric acid. When sulfuric acid was used at loadings equivalent to those of nitric acid employed for NA-CD(1) and NA-CD(2) syntheses, the trends in the PL spectra were very similar to (but more pronounced than) those observed for nitric acid (Fig. S12). This observation supported the hypothesis that nitric acid acted primarily as a proton donor.

Transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy was employed to investigate the evolution of excited states following acid treatment. Upon excitation at 320 nm, all samples exhibited a broad excited-state absorption (ESA) band spanning 375–480 nm (Fig. 5 a–c). Contour map analysis revealed a decrease in the positive ΔA signal at ∼390 nm after 1 ps in nitric acid-treated samples. Spectral analysis at various delay times confirmed the formation of a ground-state bleaching (GSB) state near 390 nm, which contributed to the observed valley-like structure in the positive signal. This GSB state, indicated by the black arrow in Fig. 5b and c, is associated with the short-wavelength PL of the samples. The depth of this valley increased with increasing nitric acid loading, directly correlating with the enhanced short-wavelength PL intensity. When excited at 350 nm, all samples displayed GSB states at 575 nm and 640 nm, contributing to long-wavelength PL (Fig. 5d–f). Overall, TAS results suggest that acid treatment promotes the formation of a state at 390 nm, suppressing the n-π∗ transition and favoring the π-π∗ transition.

Time-resolved PL (TRPL) spectroscopy was used to investigate the recombination dynamics of NISs within NA-CDs. The photocarriers were excited at different energy levels using excitation wavelengths of 310 and 530 nm (Fig. 6 and S17), and the fitting of the resulting decay curves by a biexponential decay function revealed two distinct decay processes characterized by short and long lifetimes (Table S3). The shorter time constant (τ 1 = 0.93–1.57 ns) was attributed to fast PL decay originating from heteroatomic defect states (or intragap states) associated with the NISs [48,49], whereas the longer time constant (τ 2 = 4.62–6.84 ns) represented slower PL decay assigned to more intrinsic electronic states of graphitic carbon structures. According to Table S3, τ 1 decreased with the increasing nitric acid loading for both excitation wavelengths, while τ 2 concomitantly increased. This trend well aligned with the results of UV–visible absorption and PL spectroscopic analyses. With the increasing nitric acid loading, the optical responses attributed to NISs became more pronounced, whereas those associated with the graphitic carbon structures weakened. Additionally, the overall PL lifetime increased with the increasing nitric acid loading at both excitation wavelengths. This behavior indicated the defective nature of NISs. As the nitric acid loading increased, the number of NISs decreased, which resulted in longer PL lifetimes. The increase in the overall PL lifetime with the increasing nitric acid loading observed for both excitation wavelengths further supported the presence of defect states associated with the NISs and was ascribed to the concomitant decrease in the number of NISs available for exciton recombination. Thus, we conclude that nitric acid concentration strongly influences the formation of NISs in the NA-CDs, leading to photocarrier dynamics with longer lifetimes at higher concentrations. Fig. 6c summarizes the complete photophysical processes occurring in the NA-CDs.

To demonstrate the utility of functionalized NA-CDs as light emitters in self-emissive LEDs, we fabricated LEDs using 4,4′-bis(carbazole-9-yl)biphenyl (CBP) as a host and functionalized NA-CDs (20 wt% with respect to host) as a dopant in an emitting layer (EML) (Fig. 7 a). A self-organized polymeric buffer hole-injection layer (Buf-HIL) and 2,2′,2″-(1,3,5-benzinetriyl)-tris(1-phenyl-1-H-benzimidazole) (TPBi) were used as hole-injection/transport and electron-transport layers, respectively. The Buf-HIL is composed of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) and tetrafluoroethylene–perfluoro-3,6-dioxa-4-methyl-7-octande-sulfonic acid copolymer (PFI) [50–52]. Owing to its self-organization during spin coating, PFI tended to be located on top of PEDOT:PSS. The large proportion of PFI at the top of the Buf-HIL prevented exciton quenching at the Buf-HIL/EML interface and thus increased luminescence efficiency. Under a certain electrical bias, electrons and holes were transported to the CBP:NA-CD EML through TPBi and Buf-HIL, respectively (Fig. 7b). The work function of NA-CDs was determined as 3.5 eV by ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) analysis as the difference between the excitation energy hν (21.2 eV) and spectrum edge of the secondary electrons E cutoff (Fig. S18) [53]. The organic bonds resulted in a minimally higher value, and the UPS analysis considered the lowest unoccupied and highest occupied molecular orbitals rather than the NISs, which resulted in the same value for all samples.

LEDs exhibited electroluminescence (EL) through radiative recombination in the NA-CD dopants (Fig. 7a and b). The NA-CD(0)-based LED exhibited red emission (Fig. 7c and S19), while the NA-CD(2)-based LED exhibited pink emission (Fig. 7d and S20). In detail, the NA-CD-based LEDs showed EL peaks at 595 and 645 nm at various operating voltages, which well agreed with the PL spectra (Fig. 7e and f). The high-intensity peak at ∼400 nm was attributed to the EL of the host material of the EML, i.e., CBP (Fig. S21). The NA-CD(0)-based LED exhibited a characteristic long-wavelength emission peak around 600 nm (Fig. 7e). However, when nitric acid was added, the distinct peaks at 410 and 430 nm in the PL spectrum were also observed in the EL spectrum (marked with asterisks), and the same peaks around 600 nm were observed regardless of the voltage (Fig. 7f). This indicated that acid addition suppressed the formation of pyridine/pyrrole structures and thus enhanced blue emission. The successful application of this transition to LEDs demonstrated that the NA-CDs could be used to fabricate effective multicolor LEDs.

A strategy for synthesizing N-doped CDs via the nucleophilic dehydration and intermolecular cyclization of NA (NA-CDs) was developed. Nitric acid was used to achieve the controlled protonation of the amino groups and effectively suppress the formation of N-heterocycles, such as pyrrole and pyridine. This approach enabled the precise control of nitrogen doping levels, as confirmed by the results of 1H NMR, FTIR, and XPS analyses. Consequently, the NA-CDs exhibited tunable PL properties, with the increase in nitric acid loading leading to a narrower-band blue emission centered around 400 nm and significant reduction in red emission at 600 nm. This phenomenon was described as acid-based bandgap modulation, with the acid acting as a proton donor. Our findings, further supported by the results of time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy, suggested that the long-wavelength visible emission originated from carrier trapping by NISs. The NA-CDs enabled the fabrication of high-efficiency multicolored LEDs with excellent color purity. Consequently, this study provides valuable insights into how nitrogen incorporation influences the electronic band structure and optical behavior of CDs, thus paving the way for the development of nontoxic and easily tunable phosphors for optoelectronic applications.

NA (≥99 %), ethanol (200 proof, ≥99.5 %), and toluene (anhydrous, ≥99.8 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Toluene-d8 was purchased from Cambridge Isotope Laboratories Inc. Nitric acid (68.0–70.0 %) and sulfuric acid (95.0 %) were purchased from Samchun Pure Chemical Co. Ltd. PEDOT:PSS was purchased from Heraeus. CBP and TPBi were purchased from OSM. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

NA (715.95 mg) was dissolved in ethanol (5 mL), and the solution was supplemented with nitric acid (5 M; 0, 50, 100, or 200 μL) upon stirring. The purple-red solution was transferred into a poly(p-phenol)-lined stainless-steel autoclave and placed in a convective oven. The oven was heated to 250 °C for 4 h and then cooled to room temperature. The bright red solution was dialyzed against ethanol for at least five days using Spectra/Por Biotech cellulose ester dialysis tubes with a molecular weight cutoff of 100–500 Da (Spectrum Chemical Mfg. Corp., Gardena, CA, USA). The products obtained using nitric acid loadings of 0, 50, 100, and 200 μL were denoted as NA-CD(0), NA-CD(0.5), NA-CD(1), and NA-CD(2), respectively, and were obtained in yields of 5, 7, and 10 mg, respectively.

The 1H NMR spectra of NA and NA-CDs were acquired using a Bruker Avance III HD500 spectrometer (500 MHz). Samples were prepared by completely drying 10 mg NA-CDs using a rotary evaporator and then dissolving the dried extracts in toluene-d8. FTIR spectra were recorded using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer equipped with an attenuated total reflection accessory. XPS measurements were performed using a VG Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer equipped with an Al K α X-ray source (1486.6 eV). The NA-CDs were dispersed in ethanol via sonication, and the dispersion was dropped onto a TEM grid for TEM observation. TEM imaging was performed using a JEOL JEM-2200FS instrument equipped with a Cs corrector. DLS measurements were performed using a Horiba SZ-100 particle size analyzer. HPLC was performed using Shimadzu Nexera FV with an Ultrahydrogel 250 column and a mobile phase consisting of a 1:1 v/v mixture of water and methanol (flow rate: 0.8 mL/min; injection volume: 25 μL). XRD analysis was performed using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer. Raman spectra were recorded using an Alvatek XperRam S spectrometer with 532 nm laser excitation. The NA-CD samples were deposited on pretreated Si/SiO2 substrate by spin-coating at 4000 rpm for 30 s. The UPS (AXIS Supra, Kratos) measurement was performed with a He I (21.2 eV) photon source with the beam size of 8 mm. The analysis chamber was maintained under a high vacuum condition of ∼10−7 Torr during the analysis. Each spectrum was acquired with a pass energy of 10 eV, a step size of 0.025 eV and a dwell time of 100 ms.

For all spectroscopic measurements, the NA-CDs were dispersed in ethanol by sonication. UV–visible absorption and PL spectra were recorded on a Scinco S-3100 spectrophotometer and Jasco FP-8500 fluorometer using 10 mm × 10 mm QS-grade quartz cuvettes (111-QS, Hellma Analytics), respectively. Absolute QYs were determined using a Jasco FP-8500 fluorometer with a 100 mm integrating sphere (ILF-835) and analyzed using the Jasco Spectra Manager II software.

For TA measurements, NA-CD samples were loaded into a 2 mm quartz cuvette and continuously stirred. A regeneratively amplified ytterbium-doped potassium gadolinium tungstate (Yb:KGW) laser (Pharos, Light Conversion) generated 1028 nm pump pulses with a duration of 196 fs and a repetition rate of 200 kHz. An optical parametric amplifier (Orpheus, Light Conversion) with a second harmonic module was used to tune the pump beam to wavelengths of 320 nm, 350 nm, and 480 nm, with a pump power of 4 mW. A portion of the fundamental beam served as the probe pulse, which was focused onto a sapphire plate to generate white-light continuum using a TA spectrometer (Harpia, Light Conversion) in the visible spectral range of 350–510 nm and 490–910 nm. The probe beam was then focused onto the sample, overlapping with the pump beam area. The transmitted probe beam was collected using a detector (Kymera 193i, Andor).

PL lifetime measurements were performed on NA-CD samples diluted to 0.1 mg/mL in ethanol. Samples were contained within a 2 mm quartz cuvette and continuously stirred during measurements. A regeneratively amplified ytterbium-doped potassium gadolinium tungstate (Yb:KGW) laser (Pharos, Light Conversion) with a beam radius of 100 μm and a repetition rate of 200 kHz was used for excitation. The excitation pulse was tuned to 310 nm and 530 nm with a power of 100 μW. TRPL measurements were conducted using a time-correlated single photon counting module (PicoHarp 300, PicoQuant) attached to the spectrometer (HRS-300, Princeton Instruments).

Indium tin oxide (ITO)-patterned glass slides were cleaned by sonication in acetone and 2-isopropanol for 15 min each, boiled in 2-isopropanol, and UV/ozone-treated for 10 min to make the ITO surface hydrophilic and remove residual organics. Subsequently, Buf-HIL, which incorporated PEDOT:PSS and PFI, was spin-coated on the ITO-patterned slides and baked at 150 °C for 30 min. Then, a solution of NA-CDs and CBP in tetrahydrofuran was spin-coated at 1000 rpm for 60 s in an N2-filled glovebox. Finally, TPBi (50 nm), LiF, and aluminum (80 nm) were sequentially deposited in a thermal evaporator under high vacuum (<10−7 Torr). The current–voltage–luminance characteristics were measured using a Keithley 2400 source measurement unit and Minolta CS2000 spectroradiometer.

Sehyeon Park: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Su Hwan Lee: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation. Yujin Kim: Investigation, Formal analysis. Hyeonjin Park: Validation. Gui-Min Kim: Formal analysis. Young-Hoon Kim: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Woosung Kwon: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

Woosung Kwon reports financial support was provided by National Research Foundation of Korea. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the Mid-career Research Grants (NRF-2022R1A2C4002403 and RS-2024-0035615), Digital Research Innovation Institution Program (RS-2023-00283597), Nano & Material Technology Development Program (2021M3H4A3A01062960), and Engineering Research Center (2022R1A5A1033719) funded by the National Research Foundation of Korea, the Ministry of Science and ICT. This research was partially supported by the Korea Basic Science Institute (National research Facilities and Equipment Center) Grant funded by the Ministry of Education (2021R1A6C101A564).
